steps.title: Map the poem's structure
steps.content: The first four lines promise change and renewal: new roads, fresh seeing, replenished lives. Then line 5 pivots with But: "the same backs bending, / The same sad feet shall drum." This contrast — renewal promised, yet struggle persisting — is the poem's central tension.

steps.title: Match the tension to each choice
steps.content:
The poem holds two truths simultaneously: life can feel refreshed (rewarding) and yet burdensome repetition endures (challenging).
  • Choice A — 'To consider how the repetitiveness inherent in human life can be both rewarding and challenging': Captures the dual nature the poem presents. ✓
  • Choice B — 'To question whether activities completed at one time of day are more memorable than those completed at another time of day': Imposes a day/night comparison the poem never makes. ✗
  • Choice C — 'To refute the idea that joy is a more commonly experienced emotion than sadness is': Invents a frequency argument not present in the text. ✗
  • Choice D — 'To demonstrate how the experiences of individuals relate to the experiences of their communities': The poem says nothing about community versus individual experience. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
